text:
Wonder if the chance that scaling up with Photonics will be required, and not just for the scale out is priced in.

Maine won’t be the only state to block data center builds due to energy consumption and thermal regulation advantages could drive use in scaling up if mass production can be efficiently achieved.

I’d also wonder how many of the “canceled” data centers got word that “hey your current schematics won’t work for where we are going” so it’s forced to go back to a rebid.

This stuff is fairly expensive to screw up so although CapEx is urgent it’s not without strategy.

text: The photonics cluster is what jumps out — $AAOI +332%, $AEHR +249%, $LITE +143%, $COHR +67% are all riding the same 800G/1.6T optical interconnect buildout inside hyperscaler data centers. This isn't random, it's one thesis playing out across four names. $SNDK and $WDC at #2 and #5 is also interesting, HDD/flash demand recovering hard while everyone was focused on AI chips. What's the one name on this list that still has the most room left in your view?

text:
The shift to Glass Substrates and Co-Packaged Optics is the biggest infrastructure pivot in a decade. If you trade AI, semicap, or photonics, this is required reading.

https://t.co/HjI1pTeIX0

$LPKF is interesting because it sits at the enabling layer of this transition. Its LIDE and glass-substrate systems are designed for defect-free through-glass vials, precise glass structuring, and production-scale advanced packaging, exactly the sort of process capability glass-core substrates need if they are going to matter in AI data centers and heterogeneous integration.

What makes this different from a normal “new tech” story is that the entire stack is moving at once. Hyperscalers want lower power, higher bandwidth, and tighter integration, and that pushes the industry toward optical I/O, co-packaged optics, and glass-based interposers that can support cleaner signal paths and better manufacturing scalability.

$LPKF is the machinery story. It is the precision laser-processing and glass-structuring pick that benefits if glass substrates become a standard platform rather than a niche experiment. The market often prices the chip designers and optics vendors first, but the equipment and process layer can be just as important once adoption starts.

Then you have the broader photonics names that represent different parts of the buildout. Sivers Semiconductors $SIVE operates in Wireless and Photonics, with photonics products tied to optical communication, sensing, and optical wireless networks, so it is exposed to the same bandwidth and light-based connectivity trend that supports CPO.

$IQE is another name that matters because it manufactures compound semiconductor materials and epitaxial wafers for photonics applications, including visible and infrared materials used in sensing and communications. That makes IQE part of the upstream materials layer that optical systems depend on, especially where compound semiconductors and III-V materials are central.

$POET is a more direct photonic-integration story because it is built around the POET Optical Interposer, a platform meant to integrate electronic and photonic devices into a single multi-chip module using wafer-level manufacturing and packaging. That positions POET squarely inside the AI/datacenter optical-connectivity narrative rather than adjacent to it.

$LITE and $COHR are the more established photonics names, and they matter because they make lasers, optical subsystems, and photonic technologies that power AI and cloud infrastructure. Nvidia’s recent strategic investment in both companies underscores how serious the industry is about securing next-generation optical supply for AI infrastructure.

text: @eyesigh558 Mobile is 52% of revenue and was down 36% year over year last quarter. That is exactly why the stock is down 75% from its highs and why the photonics segment is being ignored. The mobile downcycle is the noise

People are shifting to majors in $AMZN $GOOG $NVDA and also in photonics they are moving towards the majors... $CLS $COHR and $CREDO 

You can see it. People are rushing to secure gains in photonics but don't want to leave the sector of semi's. 

High beta options might struggle that have ran 100-200%+

$ALAB and $APH still have room.

text:
$SIVE

I just found out about this.

I only analyzed the photonics part.

Are you telling me that my largest position combines photonics (the booming sector) and space (the next booming sector)?

Come on.

I’m not selling anything until a 10x at the very least.
embedded_text:
Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.
New post https://t.co/2cikkV7qOg

text:
$AAOI looks a bit extended but no denying the strength both in the photonics basket as well as this name specifically. 200 should come in short order if this holds up.

Two ways to play it: retest of the 10d SMA for the lower-risk entry, or a breakout through 160.

Watch LITE, GLW and the rest of the photonics basket for confirmation, always best to see names within a group/theme move together.

text:
Frontrunning 1.6T/CPO within the broader photonics supercycle is the most compelling investment to me. 

I have high conviction in that statement.

Which is why I'm long the entire supply chain (+1 extra bottlenecK)

1. $SIVE - Their laser revenue scales aggressively with $JBL, $MRVL, Ayar, O-Net.

And I do think CPO/1.6T will blow away any conservative analyst projections from how hard $NVDA, $GOOGL, and others have been pushing photonics architectures.

Downside risk is multi-sourcing, but there's a reason Jabil chose Sivers. When you compare $MTSI, $LITE, $COHR, Furukawa, and others.

There's genuinely not many laser suppliers in the entire world... they're all $10B+, then you have this mini CHIPS act chokepoint trading at <$1B MC.

2. Shunsin (6451) - I don't see how it's possible Foxconn's optical foundry for testing, packaging, and assembly is valued at $1.5B MC less than $LWLG. 

When they look extremely derisked piggybacking off of Foxconn's photonics volume.

$TSM's optical arm VisEra example is ~$5B, but they scale H2 2028 from Gen-3. Foxconn looks to be ramping up just next year.

They're just scaling low fwd p/e multiples off of $NVDA CPO supply chain demand in Taiwan and all public indicators point to capacity expansion + extreme demand. 
 
3. Win Semi - They're the foundry for Sivers to scale up DFB laser production. As well as $AVGO, SpaceX supply chains and others.

When I do supply chain mapping and Win Semi pops up in every single frontier supply chain I see. 

There's probably something markets are not pricing in.

4. $MRVL - I find this genuinely compelling as a mini-Broadcomm. 

Their potential design with with $GOOGL today, helps the case past 2028.

But the catalyst I was looking at was $MSFT Maia ramp, which happens H2 2026, and likely keep scaling up exponentially into 2027, 2028, 2029. 

Celestial acquisition was probably the smartest thing in the world for them. Maybe on next drop or CSP? 

5. $HPS.A - Transformers/Switchgears are commodities  + boring parts of the DC supply chain.

However, when the bottleneck is 2-5 years, and you have backlog increasing 100%+... causing extreme shortages.

It's only up 20%+ since my thesis post, but I do see this being de-risked given massive backlog visibility (even though it's inferred, they don't give exact #). 

I do think markets are missing something, especially with potential gross margin expansion from price hikes if they pull it off.... 

Again backlog + demand just de-risks this company,  and it seems like a high growth compounder post facility expansion last year. 

There's many others like $NBIS, $JBL, $RPI, $TSEM, $LITE, $ARM, $SOI, $AXTI,  $IQE, $ALRIB, Fittech, PCL, and others that I'm very fond of, but just mentioning 5 off the top of my head from today's prices... if I'm creating a new portfolio. 

Of course, it's good to barbell with other uncorrelated companies to AI supply chains, but these are just 5 I liked.

{"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "THE OPTICAL PHOTONICS BOTTLENECK\n\nAs AI clusters scale past copper’s physical limits, the bottleneck shifts to optical & these are the companies building that layer across the stack: \n\n1. $AAOI building the transceiver layer of the AI network through vertically integrated U.S.-based InP laser manufacturing. It has already secured over $200M in its first volume 1.6T order from one hyperscale customer followed by another $124M in 800G orders from a second.\n\n2. $AEHR building the reliability layer for the optical & AI hardware stack through burn-in & test systems. It just received a record $41M follow-on order from its lead hyperscale customer reinforcing the idea that Sonoma is becoming a key production burn-in platform for high-power AI ASICs.\n\n3. $CRDO building the connectivity layer that helps AI clusters move data faster through active electrical cables, retimers & high-speed interconnect silicon. The DustPhotonics acquisition also extends that platform into silicon photonics before copper becomes a real constraint.\n\n4. $LITE building the laser layer of the AI optical stack through EMLs, optical components & optical switching exposure. The setup is backed by a $2B $NVDA strategic investment & optical circuit switch backlog above $400M with orders reportedly extending through 2028.\n\n5. $VIAV building the testing & validation layer of the optical stack through network instrumentation & photonics measurement tools. It is the picks-and-shovels layer of the transition because every high-speed optical buildout still needs to be tested regardless of which transceiver vendor wins.\n\n6. $COHR building one of the core photonics bottlenecks through indium phosphide lasers, optical engines & communications components tied to next-gen AI networking. It also has a $2B $NVDA strategic investment behind it & is doubling InP device capacity into the 1.6T ramp.\n\n7. $MRVL building the DSP & optical infrastructure layer through electro-optics, PAM DSPs, interconnect silicon & custom networking chips. The Celestial AI deal & NVLink Fusion exposure both strengthen its position as photonics becomes more central to AI cluster design."}}}, "rest_id": "2045885512922419251", "source": "<a href=\"https://mobile.twitter.com\" rel=\"nofollow\">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "168636", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2046003571603423359", "source": "<a href=\"https://mobile.twitter.com\" rel=\"nofollow\">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10336", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
This map shows why Sivers - ( $SIVE ) is quietly becoming one of the most important names in AI photonics.

Sivers sits in the center of the CPO ecosystem supplying the critical multi-wavelength laser arrays to:

•  Jabil (1.6T LRO pluggables)
•  Ayar Labs (optical I/O)
•  O-Net + Enablence (external light source modules)
•  POET (hybrid integration, now tied to Marvell + Google TPU discussions)

That’s direct or indirect exposure to $META , $AMZN , $MSFT , $GOOGL , and $MRVL — basically the entire hyperscaler map.

Still only ~$720M market cap

The Nasdaq listing is coming. The commercial ramps are coming.

text:
The Copper Wall Account 🧱➡️💡

My thesis: The next wave of AI infrastructure is the shift from copper interconnects to silicon photonics and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in data centers. Power efficiency and bandwidth demands are forcing the change and I’m adding exposure to quantum computing.

Here’s my initial DCA plan:

Starting tomorrow: $5/week into each of these 10 stocks 

$TSM $AVGO $MRVL $LITE $AAOI $CRDO $FN $TSEM $IONQ $HON

•  Photonics/AI infrastructure play: TSM, AVGO, MRVL, LITE, AAOI, CRDO, FN, TSEM

•  Quantum long-shot: IONQ + HON (via Quantinuum)

I’ll post weekly updates on performance, news, and any adjustments.

If the AI buildout accelerates and silicon photonics/CPO gets widely adopted, I expect strong returns by 2030.

If IONQ or Quantinuum leads in quantum, this 10-year DCA could compound beautifully by 2040.

Who’s in on the next tech wave? Drop your thoughts 👇

text:
This is the most comprehensive photonics stack there is. 

L1: Materials & Substrates

The foundation. Without this, nothing above it exists.
L1 companies grow the raw crystal wafers – InP, GaAs, Ge that silicon can’t replace for high-speed photonics.

Key names: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE, $AIXA, $COHR, $LASR

Top performer: AXT (AXTI) +6,837%

InP demand exploded for AI datacenter optical connectivity. Q3 2025 InP revenues up 250%+ sequentially. Doubling capacity by end of 2026. 

L2: Active Photonic Devices

This is where light gets made.
Lasers, modulators, photodetectors. In a CPO world, these get embedded directly into switch ASICs – no longer pluggable add-ons. That’s a structural demand shift, not a cycle.

Key names: $LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SIVEF, $AAOI, $MTSI, $SMTC

Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685%

Named NVIDIA collaborator on Spectrum-X silicon photonics. Recently received the largest single CPO laser purchase commitment in company history.

L3: Electro-Optical Connectivity ICs

The translators. Silicon that bridges optical and electrical worlds.

TIAs, drivers, DSPs, SerDes, retimers. As speeds push to 800G and 1.6T, this layer becomes a chokepoint.

Key names: $MTSI, $SMTC, $MRVL, $CRDO, $ALAB, $MXLV

Top performer: Credo (CRDO) +361%

Not a pure photonics play – but every 800G and 1.6T deployment runs through their AEC and SerDes IP.

L4: PIC Platform & IP

The brain of the photonic system.
Waveguides, modulators, and photodetectors on a single chip. Highest-IP layer in the stack. Whoever locks platform relationships here wins the next decade.

Key names: $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO, $IOLI, $EOPT (Ayar Labs, Lightmatte)

Top performer: Innolight +948%

Partnered with Tower Semi on a breakthrough SiPh platform that cuts lasers per module in half.

L5: Foundry & Process Platform

You need a fab. Not just any fab.
SiPh fabrication needs specialized processes – TFLN, SiPh COUPE, SoIC – that only a handful of foundries can run globally.

Key names: $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $TSM

Top performer: Tower Semi (TSEM) +542%

SiPh revenue hit ~$52M in Q3 2025 alone, up 70% YoY. Full-year 2025 target: $220M+. Q4 annualized run rate: $320M+. Investing $650M to triple SiPh shipments by mid-2026.

L6A: Engine & Module

Where the optical engine becomes a product – 400G, 800G, 1.6T modules.
Most commoditized layer today. CPO transition will redraw it entirely.

Key names: $COHR, $LITE, $AAOI, $FN, $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO

Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685%

Vertically integrated across L2 and L6A. Supply locked by hyperscalers. Pricing power persists.

L6B: Infrastructure

Fiber and cable. The physical nervous system of every AI cluster.
Boring until hyperscalers start spending $50B+ on datacenter campuses. Then it’s anything but.

Key names: $GLW, $OTCPK:SMOKY, $PRY, $APH, $FJKUF

Top performer: Fujikura (5803.TSE) +748%

Japanese fiber giant. Direct beneficiary of AI intra-datacenter cabling demand going parabolic.

L6C: Transport

Moves data between datacenters across metro and long-haul fiber.
As AI clusters get larger and more distributed, inter-datacenter bandwidth demand compounds. This layer doesn’t slow down.

Key names: $CIEN, $NOK, $CSCO

Top performer: Ciena (CIEN) +777%

WaveLogic coherent optics is the dominant long-haul platform in North America and Europe

L7: Test & Qualification

The final gatekeeper. Every photonic device passes through here before shipping.

Burn-in, optical test, IC test, wafer test. Often overlooked. Often the highest-margin layer.

Key names: $KEYS, $TER, $ATE, $FRT, $AEHR, $VIAV, $COHU

Top performer: Aehr Test (AEHR) +954%

Shipped the world’s first production wafer-level burn-in system for AI processors. Now diversified into silicon photonics, GaN, and hard disk drives.

Great job on putting it together  @damnang2

text:
CRDO가 DustPhotonics(이름이 왜 이래😊)를 인수한 내용을 Astera Labs가 aiXscale Photonics 인수한 내용과 버무려 글을 써봤습니다.

DustPhotonics의 광학 역량을 직접 확인하고 싶어서 역량껏 찾아봤는데, 학회 논문도 없고 coupling loss 수치도 미공개라 외부 검증에는 실패했습니다. 좀 아쉽지만 그 자체가 하나의 데이터 포인트라고 생각해서 솔직하게 다뤄봤습니다.

그리고 제가 생각한 진짜 수혜 레이어는 칩을 설계한 회사도, 칩을 산 회사도 아닙니다. 그 칩을 찍는 Foundry예요. 이걸 통해서 풀어봤습니다. paywall 뒤 내용인데 이 정도는 공개합니다😅

다들 CRDO 주가, 인수 사실에만 집중하던데, 공급망 구조로 읽으면 완전히 다른 회사가 보일 것입니다.

But the company that benefits most doesn't even appear in the headlines. $CRDO is acquiring Israeli fabless SiPh PIC company DustPhotonics for $750M cash upfront. Up to ~$1.3B including earnout. What this article covers: DustPhotonics' core L3C technology. Known specs exist, but coupling loss, alignment tolerance, and other key performance data have never been disclosed. No peer-reviewed device papers at any major conference. Not saying the tech is bad. Saying independent verification isn't possible. $ALAB acquired aiXscale Photonics in October 2025. Credo bought the PIC chip. Astera bought the fiber-chip coupling interface. Different value chain positions, same direction. Electrical connectivity companies are buying their way into optics. There's a missing variable here. DustPhotonics is fabless. They've been fabricating wafers at one specific foundry for five years. SiPh PDK switching costs are far higher than CMOS. Where does this optical internalization trend show up in the numbers first? text: $CRDO is buying DustPhotonics for $750M cash plus stock. Silicon photonics for 400G, 800G, 1.6T — with a 3.2T roadmap. That's the physical layer underneath every hyperscaler AI buildout. Stock +15% today. Credo now has SerDes + DSP + SiPho + system integration under one roof. The optical connectivity land-grab has a new winner.

What this article covers: DustPhotonics' core L3C technology. Known specs exist, but coupling loss, alignment tolerance, and other key performance data have never been disclosed. No peer-reviewed device papers at any major conference. Not saying the tech is bad. Saying independent verification isn't possible. $ALAB acquired aiXscale Photonics in October 2025. Credo bought the PIC chip. Astera bought the fiber-chip coupling interface. Different value chain positions, same direction. Electrical connectivity companies are buying their way into optics. There's a missing variable here. DustPhotonics is fabless. They've been fabricating wafers at one specific foundry for five years. SiPh PDK switching costs are far higher than CMOS. Where does this optical internalization trend show up in the numbers first? text:
Copper wires are hitting a physical wall.
As #AI models demand increases in data transfer speeds between chips, electrical resistance is becoming an insurmountable barrier.
Enter Silicon Photonics: integration of laser-driven optical comms directly onto silicon substrates. https://t.co/HmVcjDHm7G

text: Being up 50% doesn’t mean fully rerated. TSEM for example still trades at a significant discount to Western peers despite being a key player in the silicon photonics supply chain, the market hasn’t fully priced its positioning yet. Even large caps like Marvell aren’t fully rerated, the market is still pricing it as a networking chip company, not as a custom AI silicon platform. That re-rating alone is worth multiple turns on the multiple. Same logic applies to smaller names like POET, where the technology is real but institutional coverage is near zero. We’re still early on most of these

text:
Big day for Soitec! 

Up a solid 40% since we profiled it. 

A lot of the recent move is due to retail climbing into the hype around Photonics following @aleabitoreddit and @citrini being bullish on the stock.

Of all the photonics names, this probably remains my favorite reward to risk. 

Given the moat. The bombed out valuation from other segments (which will inflect soon), and the partnerships the company has with players like Shin Etsu.

Excited to hold this for longer and watch the thesis play out. 

I’m used to waiting much longer for a thesis to play out- and let it be known that we haven’t fundamentally seen it played out as of yet.

Thank you to the hive mind X community we have so many experts bringing these names to light!

text:
I’m really like that man

I top ticked OXY to the day boy. My ytd rivals all those accounts that been spamming Photonics and all those AI supply chain stocks. Yet I never owned any of those AI plays. Missing out on Bloom Energy hurts my heart after pumping it earlier much in the year. I could have been talking more shit against all those AI guys if I longed it but it’s whatever 😂, being able to have a return match the AI peddlers on this platform without any of their AI exposure, feels good in it of itself. I don’t have Serenity’s return though that dude is killing it, but I legit have zero influence in being able to pump stocks. If people actually listened to me and followed my plays I would crush his return too I believe 😂. Some can be turned off by my competitiveness but this is a zero sum game. Kill or be eaten. I didn’t make the rules, but ima play the game to not just win, but to dominate. I only feel good when it’s a blow out.

text: @ParadisLabs @ParadisLabs I've actually been doing quite a bit of research in the overall photonics sector (outside of Data Center/AI). $JEN came up in my research, I can send over my write-up if you would like. Check DMs.

@PhotonicsTrackr @PepInvestStocks Yeah $LASE doesn’t really fit the layer taxonomy - they’re an end-application laser play (industrial cutting/welding/marking) rather than a supplier into the photonics stack. Interesting angle is the pivot into defense/directed energy.

@mattpurdy89 @PepInvestStocks $LASE is interesting- we didn’t include it in our layer mapping. It does call itself a photonics-based industrial products company but we couldn’t find any evidence of it fitting into our layer taxonomy.

text:
I'm confused how Jenoptik, $JEN, is up 65% YTD?

The "Photonics" label is doing a lot of heavy lifting for some pretty shaky fundamentals:

-> $JEN is a Tier 2 supplier, but getting priced like a monopoly. High capex + R&D exposure without pricing power of true lithography giants like $ASML

-> ​€100M capex on their new fab is just going to kill margins. With the semi-recovery looking lumpy in Q2 2026, the fixed cost drag could lead to a huge earnings miss by end of yr.
​
-> ​26x P/E for a business that is still 30% low-margin traffic safety and automotive metrology. The "pure photonics" rebrand is a marketing ploy, and not a financial or operational reality yet.

-> In the Photonics segment, Chinese competitors are eroding the moat in micro-optics and laser modules. $JEN is being squeezed between the high-end (Zeiss) and the low-cost (MLOptic).

$JEN is just a cyclical hardware firm w/ significant non-core baggage being priced like a secular AI winner.

High capex + R&D exposure without pricing power of true lithography giants like $ASML -> ​€100M capex on their new fab is just going to kill margins. With the semi-recovery looking lumpy in Q2 2026, the fixed cost drag could lead to a huge earnings miss by end of yr. ​ -> ​26x P/E for a business that is still 30% low-margin traffic safety and automotive metrology. #OPIE2026 #Photonics #OpticalEngineering # #LorentzSoftware #ParticleBeam #BeamPhysics #AcceleratorPhysics #Electromagnetics #EngineeringSimulation https://t.co/K8oKymfCU2
Something Ive also highlighted with $SIVE, its more than just a photonics story.
embedded_text:
Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.
New post https://t.co/2cikkV7qOg

6. Optics: $LITE $COHR $POET $MEMS $AAOI $OPTX $LPTH
7. Networking: $CRDO $MRVL $SMTC $CIEN $NOK $GLW $ALAB $ANET $AVGO

text:
Remember, I lived next door to a confirmed robotics and photonics expert, Mr. Pierrick Vulliez. This should not be difficult to understand—even for Grok.

In the footage, the hand movements of the man standing in the trench align point-for-point with the robot digger’s shovel movements. This is one of several videos I’ve posted over the past few days suggesting how closely a human operator must be controlling the machine, and all involve precise hand or head movements by the controller from what I’ve seen.

If the trench activity were human controlling another human that would imply a level of coordination I’m not prepared to interpret beyond what my current evidence supports.

text:
The AI infrastructure bottleneck nobody talks about.

As AI clusters scale past copper's physical limits, optical photonics becomes the critical layer.

These companies are building the infrastructure layer that powers the next generation of AI systems—and the investment signals that come with them.

A massive data throughput upgrade is coming. The question is which companies capture the value.

text:
I've been quietly watching on the sideline for a couple of months now.

Everybody and their grandmas are hyping photonics (and for a very good reason, no doubt on that!) but there are truly a few people doing the actual work and a ton of parrots.

A handful of persons truly do the research and others just copy/paste it, reformulate it, ask AI to generate something they didn't even read and then post.

text:
Spot on by @Chaamacoo. 🎯

People keep trying to put $SIVE / $SIVEF in a single box. It’s not just an "AI stock", a "defense stock", or a "telecom stock." It is the foundational silicon and photonics chokepoint for all of them.

Think of them as the "Intel Inside" of the next hardware supercycle. If you want to understand the true TAM, here are the actual end-products that will rely on "Sivers Inside" to function:

🧠 AI Datacenters: Next-gen 1.6T LRO pluggable transceivers (Jabil/Marvell) & Co-Packaged Optics.

 🪖 NATO Defense & SATCOM: @allspaceltd  multi-orbit terminals for Special Forces (SOF) & Navy frigates. 

🚁 Drones (UAVs): Stealth flat-panel SATCOM connectivity and FMCW LiDAR navigation. 

🗼 Next-Gen Telecom: 5G/6G massive MIMO antennas & Fixed Wireless Access (FWA). 

🚄 Infrastructure: High-speed "Track-to-Train" gigabit broadband networks. 

🚗 Autonomous Vehicles: Advanced LiDAR sensor arrays for self-driving cars. 

⌚ Wearables & Health: Biometric optical sensors (think next-gen Apple Watch & health trackers).

You aren't buying a single theme hoping it plays out. You are buying the underlying crossroads of tomorrow's technology. The sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation could be huge. ⏳🔥
$SIVE $SIVEF #Semiconductors #Photonics

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "9200", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045961814413115759", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "6019", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Photonics is a stack! 
$AXTI $AIXA $TSEM $AEHR $LITE $GLW

Here is a clean map of the sector.
Please bookmark & share! 

A laser, a photonic chip, a finished module, and a deployed AI network all sit at different points in one long chain. 

Different companies get paid for totally different reasons and on totally different timelines.

I just updated my layer framework so you can instantly see where every major name fits that I track (and why the AI buildout lifts the whole stack).

Quick map:

Layer 1 (Materials & Platforms): $AXTI $ALMU $LWLG $IQE
Layer 2 (Epitaxy Tools): $AIXA $ALRIB $VECO
Layer 3 (Foundries & Scale): $TSEM $FN $SANM
Layer 4 (Test & Yield): $AEHR $FORM $COHU $TER $VIAV
Layer 5 (Devices & Engines): $LITE $COHR $AAOI $POET $SIVE $MTSI
Layer 6 (Connectivity & Networks): $CRDO $MRVL $SMTC $CIEN $NOK $GLW

There are many more names in the industry that didn't make this list. 

Which are you tracking the closest?

Agreed—the signals are aligning faster than consensus expects. Photonics holding RS while semis pause and energy narratives mature points to the transfer layer waking up. 

Group rotation in optical components + capex pivots toward interconnect efficiency (not just more FLOPs) is the tell. COHR’s positioning in that bandwidth chokepoint looks timely.

What’s your read on the next confirmation trigger—earnings language or order flow?

@grok It’s already happening @grok Semis are crowded.
Energy is understood. Now you’re seeing:

-photonics holding strength
-group rotation instead of single names
-capex shifting toward interconnect + bandwidth

That’s the early phase. Not obvious yet…but it’s forming.

text:
$CRDO just went from AEC king to full-stack AI connectivity platform.

Acquired DustPhotonics — now owns both the optical DSP and the silicon photonics PIC. 

That’s vertical integration of the two core components inside every optical transceiver.

What matters:

•Optical revenue expected to exceed $500M in FY27 — a net new growth engine on top of the AEC monopoly

•FY27 total revenue now guided 75%+ YoY growth

•Margins intact (mid-60s) — owning the PIC eliminates supplier margin stack

•Dust has hyperscaler design wins and a roadmap to 3.2T, ahead of the market

•Dual CPO/NPO roadmap (laser + microLED) — CRDO wins either way
The thesis just got wider. AECs for copper. Optical DSPs for networking silicon. ZeroFlap transceivers for bulletproof reliability. Standalone PICs for the $6B silicon photonics TAM by 2030.

One company. Every connection in the AI cluster. Copper to optical. Front-end to scale-up.

Top 5 Catalysts

1.June earnings — first print with DustPhotonics revenue contribution and updated FY27 guide with optical breakdown

2.DustPhotonics close (Q2 cal ’26) — removes deal uncertainty, unlocks cross-selling and integration synergies

3.ZeroFlap hyperscaler ramp — Brennan teased expanding customer traction, next update in June could name new wins

4.3.2T product cycle — Dust is ahead of market on 448 Gbps PIC, positions CRDO as the enabling platform for next-gen port speeds

5.CPO/NPO inflection — dual roadmap (laser via Dust + microLED in-house) means CRDO wins regardless of which architecture hyperscalers choose, with scale-up driving 10x connection density

text:
$CRDO management call summary on photonics M&A

Key Financial Information

Revenue guidance. 

Combined optical revenue (DSPs + ZeroFlap + standalone PICs) now expected to exceed $500M in FY27 — above prior guidance. Total FY27 revenue expected to grow in excess of 75% YoY. Sequencing: mid-single-digit sequential growth through H1, with a second-half inflection driven by the optical portfolio ramp.

Margins preserved. 

ZeroFlap optics stays within the long-term gross margin model in the mid-60s percent. Integrating the Dust PIC into ZeroFlap is margin accretive — eliminates third-party component margin stack. No shift in margin messaging despite the hardware mix shift toward transceivers.

Deal structure. 

Acquisition price not disclosed. Two-year contingency earnout based on a mix of financial metrics. Expected to close Q2 calendar 2026. Accretive in FY27 and beyond. Full OpEx and CapEx impact to be detailed at June earnings.

What Could Be Market Moving

The $500M optical number is new and above prior expectations. This reframes CRDO from a one-product AEC story into a diversified connectivity platform with a second major revenue pillar scaling fast. The Street will need to re-model FY27 upward.

75%+ total revenue growth guide is aggressive. That’s a step-up from where consensus likely sat pre-deal, and it’s being delivered while holding margin structure. Growth-at-scale with margin durability is exactly what commands premium multiples.

Standalone PIC TAM opens a new addressable market. Brennan cited analyst estimates of $6B by 2030. This wasn’t in anyone’s CRDO model before today. Dust’s backlog is “quite healthy and growing through FY27” — that’s real near-term revenue, not a roadmap promise.

3.2T readiness. Dust is already making progress at 448 Gbps per lane, ahead of the broader market. This positions CRDO as the enabling platform for the next port speed cycle before competitors are ready.
Tone of the Call

Confident and controlled. Brennan was deliberate about framing this as a natural extension of the system-level strategy rather than a pivot. No defensiveness on the margin question — Fleming handled it cleanly with no hedging. They declined to break out Dust’s standalone revenue or give granular optical segment splits, which signals they want to keep optionality on how they frame the narrative at June earnings.

The Q&A was constructive. Analysts were probing for risk (customer conflict on standalone PICs, margin dilution, qualification disruption for ZeroFlap) and management batted every one away without hesitation. Brennan’s repeated emphasis on “system-level approach” and “owning more of the stack” is the language of a company playing offense, not reacting to competitive pressure.
No sandbagging. 

The 75%+ growth and $500M optical numbers were offered proactively, not dragged out by analysts. That’s a management team that wanted the market to re-rate the story today.

text:
As of April 14, 2026 (removed some players from the list)

The AI photonics and optical infrastructure sector is showing a sharp split today, with substrate and materials plays surging while early-stage and speculative names get hammered. $SOI led the watchlist with an +11.91% move, extending a parabolic run fueled by accelerating investor conviction in Soitec's Photonics-SOI substrate business for co-packaged optics and AI datacenter interconnects. Morningstar noted that Soitec's shares rose 50% in just five trading sessions as investors priced in a bullish outlook for the firm's silicon photonics segment. Morningstar (https://t.co/djE1rX2T9F)
The broader sector is being reshaped by a clear bifurcation: capital is rotating into names with tangible substrate and test infrastructure exposure to the AI optical buildout, while pre-revenue photonic integration plays are getting punished. $POET dropped after Wolfpack Research disclosed a short position, calling the company "an obvious stock promotion" and flagging potential PFIC tax liabilities for U.S. shareholders ahead of the April 15 filing deadline. https://t.co/RRfdqNGIy4 $SIVEF fell in sympathy as the short report reinforced broader skepticism toward pre-revenue photonic chip names. Meanwhile, SPX +2.21% and NDX +1.66% confirm this is a risk-on tape where the photonics laggards are underperforming on idiosyncratic, not macro, weakness.
$SOI: +11.91%
$ATEYY: +8.65%
$LWLG: +7.20%
$BESIY: +4.82%
$AEHR: +3.84%
$TSEM: +2.38%
$MRVL: +1.90%
$COHR: +1.80%
$AXTI: +1.10%
$FORM: 0.00%
$KEYS: -0.22%
$TER: -1.22%
$GFS: -1.28%
$GLW: -1.35%
$VIAV: -1.64%
$LITE: -2.15%
$IQE: -2.65%
$CIEN: -2.68%
$AAOI: -4.41%
$ALMU: -7.36%
$POET: -8.01%
$SIVEF: -11.50%
1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
$SOI, $BESIY, $TSEM, $AXTI: The substrate tier is where capital is concentrating. SOI's parabolic move reflects the market treating Photonics-SOI wafers as a structural bottleneck for CPO adoption. BESIY (BE Semiconductor) rode the same wave on die-attach and advanced packaging exposure. TSEM continues to benefit from specialty foundry demand, while AXTI held green on InP substrate scarcity narrative despite thin volume.
$IQE, $GFS: IQE gave back -2.65% as the OTC liquidity drag persists despite the same end-market tailwinds. GFS slipped -1.28%, still digesting the auto/industrial overhang even as its RF-SOI partnership with Soitec positions it well longer term.
2. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
$COHR, $MRVL: Coherent and Marvell both green, tracking the 800G/1.6T transceiver cycle. COHR +1.80% is steady rather than explosive here, which suggests the easy money in pluggable transceiver names may already be priced.
$LITE, $CIEN, $AAOI: The transceiver and networking names that should be benefiting from the same AI optical build are instead lagging. LITE -2.15% and CIEN -2.68% are giving back recent gains. AAOI -4.41% continues to trade heavy, potentially reflecting profit-taking after its tariff-exemption-driven run.
$GLW, $VIAV: Passive optical infrastructure names drifting lower. Not broken, just not where the heat is today.
3. Semiconductor Equipment, Test and Infrastructure
$AEHR, $FORM, $KEYS, $TER, $ATEYY: AEHR +3.84% is the standout, continuing its recent strength on wafer-level burn-in demand tied to SiC and compound semi production ramps. ATEYY (Advantest) surged +8.65%, a strong signal that test infrastructure spending for advanced packaging and photonics ICs is accelerating. FORM flat, KEYS and TER slightly red but neither signaling anything concerning.
4. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
$LWLG, $ALMU, $POET, $SIVEF: LWLG bucked the early-stage selloff with a +7.20% move, potentially on renewed interest in EO polymer modulators as CPO architectures demand novel modulator solutions beyond conventional LiNbO3. ALMU -7.36% gave back gains on thin float dynamics. POET -8.01% is the direct casualty of the Wolfpack short report, which weaponized the PFIC tax angle right before the April 15 deadline. SIVEF -11.50% is the worst performer on the board, dragged down by contagion from the short seller thesis hitting the pre-revenue photonic integration cohort.

of April 14, 2026 (removed some players from the list)

The AI photonics and optical infrastructure sector is showing a sharp split today, with substrate and materials plays surging while early-stage and speculative names get hammered. $SOI led the watchlist with an +11.91% move, extending a parabolic run fueled by accelerating investor conviction in Soitec's Photonics-SOI substrate business for co-packaged optics and AI datacenter interconnects. Morningstar noted that Soitec's shares rose 50% in just five trading sessions as investors priced in a bullish outlook for the firm's silicon photonics segment. Morningstar (https://t.co/djE1rX2T9F)
The broader sector is being reshaped by a clear bifurcation: capital is rotating into names with tangible substrate and test infrastructure exposure to the AI optical buildout, while pre-revenue photonic integration plays are getting punished. $POET dropped after Wolfpack Research disclosed a short position, calling the company "an obvious stock promotion" and flagging potential PFIC tax liabilities for U.S. shareholders ahead of the April 15 filing deadline. https://t.co/RRfdqNGIy4 $SIVEF fell in sympathy as the short report reinforced broader skepticism toward pre-revenue photonic chip names. Meanwhile, SPX +2.21% and NDX +1.66% confirm this is a risk-on tape where the photonics laggards are underperforming on idiosyncratic, not macro, weakness.

1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
$SOI, $BESIY, $TSEM, $AXTI: The substrate tier is where capital is concentrating. SOI's parabolic move reflects the market treating Photonics-SOI wafers as a structural bottleneck for CPO adoption. BESIY (BE Semiconductor) rode the same wave on die-attach and advanced packaging exposure. TSEM continues to benefit from specialty foundry demand, while AXTI held green on InP substrate scarcity narrative despite thin volume.
$IQE, $GFS: IQE gave back -2.65% as the OTC liquidity drag persists despite the same end-market tailwinds. GFS slipped -1.28%, still digesting the auto/industrial overhang even as its RF-SOI partnership with Soitec positions it well longer term.
2. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
$COHR, $MRVL: Coherent and Marvell both green, tracking the 800G/1.6T transceiver cycle. COHR +1.80% is steady rather than explosive here, which suggests the easy money in pluggable transceiver names may already be priced.
$LITE, $CIEN, $AAOI: The transceiver and networking names that should be benefiting from the same AI optical build are instead lagging. LITE -2.15% and CIEN -2.68% are giving back recent gains. AAOI -4.41% continues to trade heavy, potentially reflecting profit-taking after its tariff-exemption-driven run.
$GLW, $VIAV: Passive optical infrastructure names drifting lower. Not broken, just not where the heat is today.
3. Semiconductor Equipment, Test and Infrastructure
$AEHR, $FORM, $KEYS, $TER, $ATEYY: AEHR +3.84% is the standout, continuing its recent strength on wafer-level burn-in demand tied to SiC and compound semi production ramps. ATEYY (Advantest) surged +8.65%, a strong signal that test infrastructure spending for advanced packaging and photonics ICs is accelerating. FORM flat, KEYS and TER slightly red but neither signaling anything concerning.
4. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
$LWLG, $ALMU, $POET, $SIVEF: LWLG bucked the early-stage selloff with a +7.20% move, potentially on renewed interest in EO polymer modulators as CPO architectures demand novel modulator solutions beyond conventional LiNbO3. ALMU -7.36% gave back gains on thin float dynamics. POET -8.01% is the direct casualty of the Wolfpack short report, which weaponized the PFIC tax angle right before the April 15 deadline. SIVEF -11.50% is the worst performer on the board, dragged down by contagion from the short seller thesis hitting the pre-revenue photonic integration cohort."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "core": {"user_results": {"result": {"__typename": "User", "affiliates_highlighted_label": {}, "avatar": {"image_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2010538072438669312/p9kcQKRC_normal.jpg"}, "core": {"created_at": "Tue Apr 29 20:18:12 +0000 2025", "name": "Photon Capital", "screen_name": "PhotonCap"}, "dm_permissions": {"can_dm": false}, "follow_request_sent": false, "has_graduated_access": true, "id": "VXNlcjoxOTE3MzEyNDM4ODU4NTg4MTYx", "is_blue_verified": true, "legacy": {"default_profile": true, "default_profile_image": false, "description": "Seeing tech through a different wavelength. text:
The rotation into CPO names is inevitable. From what I am reading, it may be coming faster than most expect.

Soitec $SLOIF up 10% today. It is the only company on earth supplying the substrate TSMC, GlobalFoundries, STMicro, and Intel use for silicon photonics at volume. There is no second source.

Every pluggable transceiver shipping into AI data centers today uses 50mm2 of it. Every CPO engine uses 200mm2. Same monopoly. 4x the content per device.

The CPO market goes from essentially nothing today to $15 billion by 2030 (263% CARG). Soitec's Photonics-SOI revenue is already approaching €86 million in FY26 growing 25% annually. That is before the CPO ramp.

The Photonics Ecosystem: Explained

Every segment, group, and notable stock within below. you'll want to bookmark this...

UPSTREAM

- Materials: $GLW $AXTI $ALMU $LWLG
- Wafer: $TSM $GFS $TSEM $SKWT 

MIDSTREAM 

- Optical Components: $HIMX $LPTH $OPTX $MTSI
- Lasers & Light: $LITE $IPGP $COHR $LASR
- Transceivers: $AAOI (+ others) 
- Packaging: $FN $AMKR $POET 

DOWNSTREAM 

- Switch & Interconnect: $AVGO $MRVL $CRDO $NVDA
- Networking: $JNPR $CIEN $CSCO $ANET 
- Testing: $AEHR $ONTO $VIAV $FORM 

I made this tool to help me understand the segmentation of the photonics world. I am not a photonics expert. If I missed anything, or need to move something around, let me know!

text:
Healthy rotation, the weakness you see in Photonics & Space themes happens when the leaders led the charge last week and were very extended.

That's why having a balanced PF and not be overly concentrated in one or a few groups is key to handle these normal & healthy rotations.

text:
A lot of people seem to think photonics has already had its big run.
They’re missing the next leg.

Stocks like $AAOI and $LITE exploded on the current 800G pluggable wave — a lot of that growth is now priced in.

The real next chapter is CPO - (Co-Packaged Optics).

CPO solves the massive power and heat problems in AI data centers way better than today’s pluggables. Hyperscalers are already designing their future clusters around it. The big ramp will be 2027–2028.

The companies that actually enable CPO — $SIVE (critical lasers), $SOI (substrates), $TSEM (foundry), $AEHR (testing) — are still trading at valuations that don’t reflect how big this shift can become.

CPO is the layer where the next major move in photonics will come from IMO.

text:
Been digging through European photonics the last few weeks.

Most of you already know $AIXA and $SOI. Both still have room.

Getting a lot of questions about $LPK.DE lately so I want to address it directly.

LPKF is the laser process pioneer for glass substrates. Intel, Samsung, and TSMC are all piloting glass as the next-generation AI packaging material. Glass handles heat, density, and integrated optics better than anything else on the roadmap.

The bottleneck is simple. Glass cracks when you drill it. LPKF's LIDE process is one of a very small number of technologies that can structure glass at production scale without cracking. No glass substrate ecosystem gets built without a tool like theirs in the line.

Management's own words on the recent call: the question is no longer whether glass breakthrough in advanced packaging happens, but when the ramp-up begins.

There are two more names attached to this supply chain that I am still researching and not ready to publish on yet.

One is the integrated metrology sitting inside $AIXA's reactors. Trades like a sleepy industrial despite the direct AI attachment.

One is the micro-cap making the actual glass wafers. Up 200% already and still small enough that real revenue ramps break it wide open.

Besi trades at 77x EBITDA on the advanced packaging theme. These trade at fractions of that one layer upstream.

H/T to @AlmaCap114204 who found this one early.

More to come.

Most of you already know $AIXA and $SOI. Both still have room. Getting a lot of questions about $LPK.DE lately so I want to address it directly. LPKF is the laser process pioneer for glass substrates. Intel, Samsung, and TSMC are all piloting glass as the next-generation AI packaging material. Glass handles heat, density, and integrated optics better than anything else on the roadmap. The bottleneck is simple. Glass cracks when you drill it. LPKF's LIDE process is one of a very small number of technologies that can structure glass at production scale without cracking. No glass substrate ecosystem gets built without a tool like theirs in the line. Management's own words on the recent call: the question is no longer whether glass breakthrough in advanced packaging happens, but when the ramp-up begins. There are two more names attached to this supply chain that I am still researching and not ready to publish on yet. One is the integrated metrology sitting inside $AIXA's reactors. Trades like a sleepy industrial despite the direct AI attachment. One is the micro-cap making the actual glass wafers. Up 200% already and still small enough that real revenue ramps break it wide open. Besi trades at 77x EBITDA on the advanced packaging theme. These trade at fractions of that one layer upstream. H/T to @AlmaCap114204 who found this one early. text:
And another one!!

$TER Teradyne has acquired TestInsight, a leader in semiconductor test development, validation, and conversion software.

So they first acquired my former employer Quantifi Photonics 🇳🇿, then my other former employer MutliLane 🇱🇧 in a JV, and now TestInsight 🇮🇱.

Teradyne picked up TestInsight, a software outfit that has been around since 1999 and sits right between chip design and ATE production test (pattern conversion, validation and virtual testing). 

They have been the default software partner for both Teradyne and Advantest (sayonara 👋🏻) since 2001, which covers roughly 90% of the SoC test market. 

For the MultiLane JV and Quantifi, the read is complementary, not overlapping: Quantifi brought optical test, MultiLane handles high-speed electrical and SerDes, and TestInsight now adds the upstream software layer. Lined up like that, it is a pretty clean roll-up aimed at owning the full test stack for AI accelerators, switch ASICs and CPO. 

Advantest $ATEYY is the one that loses here. They just watched a neutral partner get absorbed by their main competitor.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Full press release from Teradyne below:

text:
On $CRDO's acquisition of Dust Photonics.

The $TSEM foundry thesis is gaining traction, and it makes sense given Tower's SiPh capabilities.

Worth adding: $ALAB did the same thing last October, acquiring aiXscale Photonics. A copper-first interconnect company buying a small photonics startup to get into optics. Both companies made the same call.

I haven't seen anyone put these two side by side yet.

https://t.co/6ZZhAm3XsA
embedded_text:
Excellent episode. Don't feel bad if you didn't know about Dust Photonics pre-deal. They said $TSEM was probably the fab for Dust, so if $CRDO blows out the product, it is incremental demand for TSEM. Also, an interesting liquid cooling angle.

As it comes to CPU, my @openclaw

Everyone talks about $SIVE and AI photonics.

But few talk about this:
They lost a SATCOM RFQ to MixComm… then acquired them a year later.

Now they sit across: 
• AI compute (photonics) 
• SATCOM/Wireless (defense, satellite, 5G/6G, DoD) 

Two mega trends. One story.

text:
源杰科技（https://t.co/ZBjZH3oFsS）是激光芯片IDM厂商，2013年1月由张欣刚（ZHANG XINGANG，美国籍，1970年生）在陕西咸阳创立。

他清华本科、南加州大学材料科学背景，曾任Luminent研发经理、Source Photonics研发总监。现任董事长兼总经理。

妹妹张欣颖任董事（持股约3.43%）。核心技术副总潘彦廷等。

主营2.5G-50G磷化铟激光芯片，用于数据中心、5G、FTTH等。2022年科创板上市，近期AI算力需求推高股价超茅台成A股新股王。官网：https://t.co/ekEJYAQGbk

The photonics move has been sharp. A lot of names already up big.

Still watching $LITE, $AAOI and $SMTC for cleaner entries. Rather buy consolidation than chase strength.
The AI networking theme isn't done. Just waiting for the right setup.

Anyone else still in this space? https://t.co/CrtRZYLS0I

The partnerships are promising but not yet validated commercially.

Built with RTP (Rubidium Titanyl Phosphate) crystal technology, this Q-switch delivers:
🔹 High damage threshold
🔹 Fast switching speeds
🔹 Excellent thermal stability
🔹 No piezoelectric ringing

text:
$AXTI AXT Inc just announced a public offering — size and price not disclosed yet.
Proceeds go toward expanding indium phosphide substrate capacity at its China subsidiary Beijing Tongmei for global export.
InP is critical for data center silicon photonics and 5G infrastructure. Real demand story.
But the catch: AXT has been battling China export permit delays on InP shipments. This raise is to scale capacity that is still export-restricted.
Q1 2026 earnings drop April 30. Dilution risk near-term.
Not financial advice.

text:
Interesting take on the interconnect bottleneck. Photonics is indeed the physics play for scaling beyond copper limits—lower latency, power per bit, and heat as racks densify for AI training/inference. 

Data movement at exascale is the next constraint after raw FLOPs. COHR's optical tech sits right in that transfer layer. Capital cycles rotate to the real chokepoints. What's your edge on timing the shift?

Because copper-based systems hit limits:

-Heat
-Signal loss
-Power inefficiency
 
Photonics solves that. It allows data centers to scale without melting down. That’s where $COHR sits.
Right inside the transfer layer.

This is physics. You can’t scale AI if data can’t move efficiently.

text:
As models scale, something breaks:

-Bandwidth
-Latency
-Power per bit

That’s the real constraint forming. 
This is where photonics matters.

Light moves data faster
-More efficiently
-With less heat

That’s the shift most people haven’t priced in yet:
electrical → optical

text:
With a background in atomic precision measurements, I'm more optics, quantum sensing, and photonics "focused" in my current role, obviously for other applications, but thought if there was an intersection somewhere, some of the signatures of the Chronoflux could be probed with the sensors under development.

I haven't worked on Casimir devices yet, but they are on my radar. I am interested in phononic photonic resonators, especially in relation to tests of Orch OR, as well as PIC frequency combs for other sensing applications. So there's definitely some overlap.

Would be nice if you could implement the equations function in this document 🙏

The two Substack articles profile Nynomic AG as a German photonics holding company with LayTec as its key subsidiary, a quasi-monopolist in in-situ metrology for MOCVD reactors and etch processes powering AI data center lasers (InP) and power electronics (GaN). They highlight restructuring savings lifting 2026 margins to 6-8%, vertical integration across the value chain, and massive TAM expansion from optical transceivers and co-packaged optics. My analysis sees strong moats from proprietary databases, OEM lock-ins with Aixtron (90%+ share), and recurring software upsells despite past guidance misses and cyclical risks. Growth potential is substantial at 3-5x for LayTec revenue by 2028 driven by AI buildout, with Nynomic re-rating to peer multiples implying 2-3x stock upside. This stems from validated photonics bottlenecks via NVIDIA investments and staggered order cycles already showing Q4 2025 inflection.

text:
$GFS GlobalFoundries Inc. (GFS) Q4 FY2025 Post-Earnings Debrief

https://t.co/vZ2dYs7a7E

Thesis: GlobalFoundries delivered a Q4 FY2025 print that pushes the underwriting debate away from a mature-node recovery and toward whether silicon photonics, optical-module and advanced-packaging capabilities, CID growth, and geographically resilient specialty capacity can support a structurally higher-growth and higher-margin model. The strongest proof that this shift is already happening is that 2025 was the first full year with more than 60% of revenue outside smart mobile devices and automotive plus CID reached a record one-third of revenue, while elevated FY2026 capex and lower FCF conversion remain the central execution risk.

-Q1 2026 revenue, gross margin, and diluted EPS versus the $1.625B / 27% / $0.35 guide framework
-Investor Day detail on the silicon photonics bridge, including pluggables versus CPO timing, module-yield milestones, and the 40% gross-margin path
-Whether silicon photonics remains overwhelmingly pluggables-driven in 2026 while keeping the end-2028 $1B run-rate target credible
-Whether Malta optical-module flow, advanced packaging, and 200mm/300mm dual-region capacity support faster revenue conversion without yield disruption
-Whether the structural mix shift, now above 60% non-mobile revenue and one-third auto plus CID, keeps widening in 2026
-Whether government-support offsets, customer prepayments, and corridor-level debottlenecking keep FY2026 net capex and FCF conversion within the guided algorithm

(GFS) Q4 FY2025 Post-Earnings Debrief https://t.co/vZ2dYs7a7E Thesis: GlobalFoundries delivered a Q4 FY2025 print that pushes the underwriting debate away from a mature-node recovery and toward whether silicon photonics, optical-module and advanced-packaging capabilities, CID growth, and geographically resilient specialty capacity can support a structurally higher-growth and higher-margin model. The strongest proof that this shift is already happening is that 2025 was the first full year with more than 60% of revenue outside smart mobile devices and automotive plus CID reached a record one-third of revenue, while elevated FY2026 capex and lower FCF conversion remain the central execution risk. -Q1 2026 revenue, gross margin, and diluted EPS versus the $1.625B / 27% / $0.35 guide framework -Investor Day detail on the silicon photonics bridge, including pluggables versus CPO timing, module-yield milestones, and the 40% gross-margin path -Whether silicon photonics remains overwhelmingly pluggables-driven in 2026 while keeping the end-2028 $1B run-rate target credible -Whether Malta optical-module flow, advanced packaging, and 200mm/300mm dual-region capacity support faster revenue conversion without yield disruption -Whether the structural mix shift, now above 60% non-mobile revenue and one-third auto plus CID, keeps widening in 2026 -Whether government-support offsets, customer prepayments, and corridor-level debottlenecking keep FY2026 net capex and FCF conversion within the guided algorithm"}}}, "rest_id": "2045928117240541286", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "12587", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$CRDO - BAAAMMMMM!!!💥💥

In the post: why the price surged, plan & execution, entry with TP and SL and some fundamentals. Enjoy!

First of all, the reason for the strong developements in the last days was the takeover of Silicon-Photonics for 750 Mio. USD plus ~920.000 own shares (worth ~123 Mio. USD). In general the guidance for 2026 is very well.

The company operates through the segments high-speed Ethernet chips, rack-to-rack AI cables and high-bandwith requirements of data centers.

Chartwise, we'll have final bullish confirmation above the 193.5$ mark.
However the bottom is very likely to be in.
In the next days, when the interm top is build out, a very short entry-zone will follow for the late buyers like me (follow to not miss it again)📈

If the price falls below 86.49$ (SL), the bearish scenario alt. A gets activated and the correction extends longer and further to the downside. However with a price target of +400$, this risk is outweighted over long-term.

What do you think?✍️
Next update will be with the exact entry zone...

text:
Grande movimento na infraestrutura de IA 👇Mercado está investindo, e você?

A Credo $CRDO está adquirindo a DustPhotonics por US$ 750 milhões em dinheiro + ações, trazendo a tecnologia de Silicon Photonics totalmente para dentro de casa.

Por que isso importa?

Os clusters de IA estão escalando rapidamente. À medida que as velocidades avançam de 400G → 800G → 1.6T e além, a conectividade óptica se torna o gargalo e isso é oportunidade para se investir. 

A DustPhotonics desenvolve ICs fotônicos de silício (PICs) que integram funções ópticas essenciais em um único chip:

• Menor consumo de energia
• Menor custo em escala
• Maior confiabilidade
• Pronto para 3.2T

Ao combinar os PICs da DustPhotonics com os SerDes, DSPs e os ZeroFlap Optical Transceivers, a Credo passa a ter uma stack verticalmente integrada — do cobre ao óptico, do chip ao cluster.

O objetivo? Controlar uma fatia maior da camada de conectividade da IA.

A empresa espera que seu portfólio óptico ultrapasse US$ 500 milhões em receita no ano fiscal de 2027, sinalizando forte tração entre hyperscalers.

Com os investimentos em infraestrutura de IA acelerando, a interconexão óptica está se tornando um campo de batalha estratégico.

IA não é só sobre GPUs.
É sobre como você conecta elas.
